draft.introduction: The Japji Sahib is the opening composition of the Guru Granth Sahib and the most important prayer in Sikhism. Composed by Guru Nanak, it is recited daily by devout Sikhs and lays out the entire spiritual cosmology of the tradition.

translation.text: One Universal Creator God. Truth is His Name. Creative Being, Without Fear, Without Hatred, Timeless Image, Beyond Birth, Self-Existent. By the Guru's Grace. Recite! True in the beginning, True through the ages, True even now, says Nanak: Truth shall ever be. By thinking, He cannot be reduced to thought, even by thinking hundreds of thousands of times. By remaining silent, inner silence is not obtained, even by remaining lovingly absorbed. The hunger of the hungry is not appeased, even with the loads of the worlds. By cleverness, no one has obtained Him. How then can the Truth be known? How can the veil of falsehood be torn away? By walking, says Nanak, in the Will of the Commander, which is written from the very beginning.

translator_notes.note: The opening formula Ik Onkar ("One Universal Creator") is the foundational statement of Sikh theology. The passage systematically eliminates every human strategy for reaching God—thought, silence, worldly success, cleverness—and concludes that only surrender to the hukam (divine will, cosmic order) opens the path. This establishes the Sikh emphasis on grace over human effort.
